coredata.dc:title: Toward visual chiral recognition of amino acids using a wide-range color tonality ratiometric nanoprobe
coredata.dc:creator: Jafar-Nezhad Ivrigh, Zahra
Fahimi-Kashani, Nafiseh
Morad, Razieh
Jamshidi, Zahar
Hormozi-Nezhad, Mohammad Reza
coredata.dc:description: Chiral recognition has long been a challenging issue to deal with in biological systems, drug design and food authentication. Implementing nanoparticle-based probes with intrinsic or induced chirality in this field has addressed several issues concerning sensitivity, reliability, rapidness and the cost of chiral sensing platforms. Yet, research into chiral nanoprobes that can be used for visual monitoring of chiral substances is still in its infancy. As part of this study, a visual chiral recognition platform has been developed in which a combination of blue-emitting carbon dots (BCDs) and mercaptopropionic acid-capped CdTe quantum dots (MPA-QDs) with inherent chiroptical activity were employed for enantiomeric detection. The ratiometric probe displayed unique fluorescence response patterns in the presence of arginine (Arg) and histidine (His) enantiomers. Upon addition of l-amino acids, successive enhancement and quenching of emission intensity as well as a red-shift in emission wavelength of MPA-QDs were observed. The emission color of the nanoprobe changed from green to pink-red and green to brick-red red by increasing the concentration of L-Arg and L-His, respectively. In contrast, their d-amino acid equivalents have a negligible influence on the emission color and fluorescence signal of the developed nanoprobe. Due to the enantioselective vibrant color changes of the nanoprobe, RGB analysis was applied for the determination of enantiomeric excess (ee) in racemic mixture with satisfactory results, allowing smartphone-based onsite visual evaluation of ee (%). Circular dichroism, lifetime, size distribution and ζ-potential measurements were employed to study the chiroselective responses. First-principle calculations were also carried out with density functional theory (DFT) to confirm the experimental observation. Furthermore, chiroselective response patterns of the ratiometric nanoprobe were manipulated to construct a logic gate system mimicking AND, OR, and INHIBIT functions. The capability of the proposed chiral platform in visual monitoring of the fraction of enantiomers in racemic mixtures has a great potential for rapid and onsite visual discrimination of chiral compounds in the field of clinical diagnostics and drug analysis.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chiral recognition has long been a challenging issue to deal with in biological systems, drug design and food authentication. Implementing nanoparticle-based probes with intrinsic or induced chirality in this field has addressed several issues concerning sensitivity, reliability, rapidness and the cost of chiral sensing platforms. Yet, research into chiral nanoprobes that can be used for visual monitoring of chiral substances is still in its infancy. As part of this study, a visual chiral recognition platform has been developed in which a combination of blue-emitting carbon dots (BCDs) and mercaptopropionic acid-capped CdTe quantum dots (MPA-QDs) with inherent chiroptical activity were employed for enantiomeric detection. The ratiometric probe displayed unique fluorescence response patterns in the presence of arginine (Arg) and histidine (His) enantiomers. Upon addition of l-amino acids, successive enhancement and quenching of emission intensity as well as a red-shift in emission wavelength of MPA-QDs were observed. The emission color of the nanoprobe changed from green to pink-red and green to brick-red red by increasing the concentration of L-Arg and L-His, respectively.

In contrast, their d-amino acid equivalents have a negligible influence on the emission color and fluorescence signal of the developed nanoprobe. Due to the enantioselective vibrant color changes of the nanoprobe, RGB analysis was applied for the determination of enantiomeric excess (ee) in racemic mixture with satisfactory results, allowing smartphone-based onsite visual evaluation of ee (%). Circular dichroism, lifetime, size distribution and ζ-potential measurements were employed to study the chiroselective responses. First-principle calculations were also carried out with density functional theory (DFT) to confirm the experimental observation. Furthermore, chiroselective response patterns of the ratiometric nanoprobe were manipulated to construct a logic gate system mimicking AND, OR, and INHIBIT functions. The capability of the proposed chiral platform in visual monitoring of the fraction of enantiomers in racemic mixtures has a great potential for rapid and onsite visual discrimination of chiral compounds in the field of clinical diagnostics and drug analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chiral recognition is a delicate subject of study, particularly in the chemical, biological, food, agriculture, and pharmaceutical fields. Among a wide range of chiral compounds, amino acids with two D- and L-configurations play essential roles in many physiochemical processes. Due to the chiroselective behavior of many biological systems, enantiomers of amino acids exhibit different biological activities, having many physiochemical properties in common though [1–5]. Abnormalities in the concentration of L and D amino acids in the human body are also symptoms of several illnesses [6–8]. Only one enantiomer exhibits desired pharmacological effect in the case of drugs, while its antipode may have no therapeutic effect or even may have toxicities [9]. In this regard, biocatalytic synthesis of enantiopure amino acids is of high significance in the development of pharmaceuticals [10]. Therefore, the determination of enantiopurity of a chiral drug, on the one hand, and chiral amino acid intermediates, on the other hand, are highly desirable in the drug development and approval process, reducing the potential risks of the drugs of interest. Similarly, in food authentication, it is essential to monitor the enantiomeric excess of the ingredients since D-enantiomers in foodstuff differ radically from their enantiomeric counterparts in terms of taste, flavor and antimicrobial and antiaging properties [11,12]. Therefore, the development of chiral recognition platforms is a topic of broad concern, requiring the enormous efforts of researchers and manufacturing industries.

Standard separation methods, including high-performance liquid chromatography [13–15] and electrophoresis [16–18] and extraction techniques [19–21], are widely utilized for chiral sensing. Numerous electrochemical [22–30], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [31,32], colorimetric [33–42] and flourimetric [43–52] and chemiluminescence [53] and optoelectronic nose [54] analytical approaches have also been implemented for chiral discrimination of amino acids. Despite high efficacy, their applicability is highly limited due to time-consuming, costly and laborious procedures regarding the preparations of chiral selectors for differentiation of enantiomers. Moreover, they often suffer from a lack of ability in visual tracking of enantiomers. Hence, the development of simple, cost-effective and reliable chiral sensing platforms for enantiomeric discrimination is vastly required, a demanding task, though.

Integration of nanomaterials has provided a new avenue in chiral sensing approaches. Regarding the outstanding and ease of fine-tuning of their surface-related properties, considerable efforts have been devoted to the preparation of nanomaterials capable of enantiomeric recognition of chiral molecules of interest [55–58]. The optical activity of nanoparticles may attribute to intrinsic chiral defects in their nanocrystal or even asymmetry in their electronic structure [59–61]. Modification of the surface of NPs with desired chiral selectors is another strategy for inducing chiroptical activity of the nanostructures, making them ideal for enantiomeric recognition [62,63]. The spatial chiral arrangement of achiral NPs has also been reported for the fabrication of chiral nanostructures [64,65]. Despite significant advances in the design of NP-based chiral probes, several issues still need to be addressed. Fabrication of chiral nanomaterials, as well as visual tracking of enantiomeric excess, are the most pressing challenges in the design of a chiral assay [38,40,66].

Pushing the boundaries of chiral recognition, we have proposed a label-free ratiometric nanoprobe for enantiomeric discrimination of arginine and histidine, using a combination of MPA-QDs and BCDs. In the presence of L and d-amino acids of Arg and His, distinctive response patterns of the ratiometric probe have been observed, providing vibrant color changes in the emission of the nanoprobe, based on which visual discrimination of enantiomers of interest is successfully achieved. Furthermore, the determination of enantiomeric excess in racemic mixtures of arginine and histidine has provided wide emission color tonalities, making the design a promising platform for visual monitoring of D and l-amino acids using a smartphone.

D- and l-amino acids of cysteine (Cys), tryptophan (Trp), arginine (Arg), alanine (Ala), histidine (His), proline (Pro) and tyrosine (Tyr), tellurium powder, mercaptopropionic acid (TGA), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), cadmium chloride (CdCl2.2H2O), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), sodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), citric acid and urea were obtained from Merck. Milli-Q grade water, with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm−1, was used in all the experiments.

Fluorescence measurements were performed by a Xe lamp-equipped Varian Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer. UV–Vis spectra were obtained on a PerkinElmer (Lambda25) spectrophotometer. A quartz cuvette with a 1 cm path length was used in both experiments. Lifetime measurement was carried out using a time-correlated single-photon-counting spectrofluorometer system on an EDINBURG INSTRUMENTS EPL_375 at maximum emission peak. FT-IR spectra were recorded using a Cary-670 spectrometer. CD spectra were obtained using a JASCO spectropolarimeter Model J-810. The particles' size distribution histograms and ζ-potential diagrams were obtained using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS (ZEN3600). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded using the HRTEM Zeiss Libra instrument. A smartphone camera (iPhone XS Max with a 16-megapixel camera, manual mode without macros and filters) was used to take the images from 96-well microplates under a 365 nm UV lamp in a UV lightbox 32 × 25 cm in size and dimensions. The original XS Camera App was used to capture the images. The distance and angle of the smartphone's camera from the testing zone were 10 cm and 0°, respectively. A schematic view of the UV box under which the images were acquired is shown in Fig. S1. RGB values were extracted and averaged from 10 pixels of each photo using the IOS application of Color name AR.

MPA-capped CdTe QDs with yellow emission were prepared based on a previously reported approach with slight modifications. In summary, 2.9 mmol of MPA was added to 10 mL of 0.29 M CdCl2 solution to prepare Cd precursors. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 9.0 by NaOH solution in a final volume of 10.0 mL. Tellurium powder (0.8 mmol) and NaBH4 (5.0 mmol) were separately added to 75.0 mL of DI water under vigorous stirring at argon atmosphere in a three-neck round bottom flask at 50 °C. After 20 min, 10 mL of the pH-adjusted CdCl2 solution previously stirred under argon flow for 5 min was added to the above bright purple solution, followed by increasing the temperature to 150 °C. The MPA-capped CdTe QDs with a yellow emission of 580 nm and an excitation wavelength of 365 nm was allowed to cool in an ice bath. The as-prepared yellow CdTe QDs were then dialyzed for 24 h for purification. The resulting solution was then diluted in a 1:5 ratio with DI water and stored in dark condition at 4 °C for future experiments.

BCDs were synthesized according to a facile one-step hydrothermal route. Briefly, 0.3 g citric acid and 0.3 g urea were dissolved in deionized water (10 mL), followed by ultrasonication for 10 min. Then, the mixture solution was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. The product containing a light green BCDs solution was purified by a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. Typically, quantum dots are nanocrystals with a diameter of less than 6 nm. Both AFM and TEM can adequately characterize these nanoparticles. Due to the comparable resolution of AFM images in Z-axis and TEM images [67], the Z-axis dimension of the AFM image in Fig. S2D displays a scale of 22 nm, and the image's color profile reveals that the peaks are often less than 5 nm, corroborating the size distribution derived from the TEM image in Fig. S2A. The as-prepared CDs with blue emission at 450 nm (Fig. S2B) were diluted to 1:800 with deionized water and stored at 4 °C for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the ratiometric nanoprobe and fluorescence measurements
ce:section.ce:para: 50 μL solution of diluted BCDs and 100 μL solution of diluted MPA-QDs were mixed entirely in a total volume of 2.0 mL. The fluorescence intensity ratio at wavelengths of 580 and 440 nm (I580/I440) was defined as 4:1 to obtain wide color tonalities. Fluorescence spectra were recorded from 400 to 700 nm at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, and the slit-width of excitation and emission were set to 10 nm. The enantiomeric detection was performed by adding 100 μL of D or L amino acids with various concentrations to the as-prepared ratiometric probe. Fluorescence spectra were recorded after 30 min incubation. The ratio of maximum emission intensity of MPA-QDs and fluorescence intensity of BCDs at 440 nm was used for quantitative measurements.

To further evaluate the applicability of the proposed chiroselective platform for the determination of enantiomeric excess in racemic mixtures, a series of racemic mixtures of Arg and His with different mole fractions were added to the as-prepared ratiometric probe. Fluorescence analysis was performed on the samples (in triplicate trials), similar to the procedure mentioned above.

Scheme 1 illustrates the sensing strategy of the proposed ratiometric probe for enantioselective discrimination of chiral amino acids. The chiral discrimination capability of the proposed assay was investigated upon interaction with L and D enantiomers of Arg and His, evaluating distinct response profiles allowing their discrimination. BCDs with a fluorescence emission maximum at 450 nm (Fig. S2) were utilized in the design of the ratiometric probe to provide wide color tonalities, enabling visual discrimination of the amino acid enantiomers. BCDs were used as an internal standard as well to correct analyte-independent factors influencing the fluorescence signal. Thus, the measurement of the ratiometric signal enhanced the sensitivity and improved the quantification capability of the proposed method [68,69]. The mixture of BCDs and yellow-emissive MPA-QDs at a fluorescence ratio of 1:5 produces a green emission color (Fig. S3) with characteristic emission peaks at 450 nm and 580 nm. The ratiometric nanoprobe shows distinctive fluorescence patterns in the presence of the enantiomers of arginine and histidine. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the fluorescence emission of MPA-QDs significantly enhances upon the addition of both arginine enantiomers; however, l-arginine (L-Arg) caused a red-shift in the emission spectrum MPA-QDs, while the emission peak of QDs slightly shifted in the presence of d-Arginine (D-Arg). The presence of dangling bonds and surface imperfections on the surface of the QDs facilitates non-radiative recombination, resulting in a lower quantum yield. Complexation of incompletely bound cadmium ions on the QDs surface by the amino group in Arg minimizes such surface defect sites by efficiently eliminating the trap states, boosting QDs passivation and improving the intensity of photoluminescence emission [70]. These results provide various emission color alterations of the ratiometric nanoprobe upon the addition of L-Arg (from green to pink-red) and D-Arg (from green to yellowish-green), enabling visual enantioselective differentiation of Arginine enantiomers. The fluorescence emission of BCDs remains almost unchanged in the presence of either L-Arg or D-Arg amino acids. On the other hand, histidine enantiomers oppositely cause fluorescence quenching of the nanoprobe. As shown in Fig. 1B, a simultaneous quenching and red-shift in the emission spectrum of QDs upon the addition of l-histidine (L-His) produce color tonalities from green to brick-red, while a slight decrease in the fluorescence signal of QDs is observed in the presence of d-Histidine (D-His) enantiomer. Due to the photoinduced electron transfer (PET), the presence of L-His in the solution triggered fluorescence quenching. Amino groups are ideally suited as electron donors for the PET process. In the presence of L-His with a greater affinity for the surface of QDs, PET can occur between the nitrogen lone pair and QDs, resulting in quenching [71,72]. Considering the substantial dependence of PET on the electron donor–fluorophore distance, PET can be repressed (in the case of L-His) or enhanced (in the case of Arg), hence boosting or quenching the fluorescence of QDs. The distinctive behavior of the enantiomers towards MPA-QDs is ascribed to the different affinity of the enantiomers to the surface of QDs, which is further investigated using DFT modeling. The fluorescence quenching of BCDs is also observed in the presence of L-His, while there is no change upon the addition of D-His. The fluorescence quenching of BCDs and YQDs and strong red-shift in emission wavelength of YQDs make visual recognition of L-Arg and L-His possible. This characteristic behavior of the ratiometric nanoprobe provides an effective platform for enantioselective discrimination of arginine and histidine enantiomers.

The morphology and particle size of CdTe QDs characterized by HRTEM and DLS measurements are shown in Fig. 2 A. According to HRTEM images, the as-prepared MPA-QDs reveal the presence of almost monodispersed spherical morphologies with an average diameter of 5 nm. At higher magnification, clear crystal lattice fringes of a single dot in the HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 2A) represent a high crystallinity of CdTe QDs with a fringe spacing distance of around 0.4 nm. Inconsistent with HRTEM results, DLS measurement also confirms the formation of MPA-QDs with a relatively uniform narrow size distribution of less than 5 nm. The ζ-potential of the MPA-QDs was −3.5 mV, indicating that the carboxylate (COO−) groups of MPA (pKa = 4.34) were exposed on the surface of QDs. Fig. 2B depicts UV–Vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of MPA-QDs in an aqueous solution. MPA-QDs exhibit strong absorption at 450 nm and a characteristic emission band at 580 nm under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The as-synthesized MPA-QDs show yellow emission under UV-light irradiation. The successful fabrication of MPA-QDs was also investigated by FT-IR analysis. Fig. 2C displays the FT-IR spectra of MPA and MPA-capped CdTe QDs. The characteristic absorption peaks of 3400 cm-1 and 1700 cm-1 are attributed to stretching vibration O–H and CO bonds. The position and features (750 - 1750 cm–1) of FT-IR spectral peaks for MPA and MPA-QDs are similar; however, the S–H vibration peak of MPA (around 2700 cm−1) has disappeared in the FT-IR spectrum of MPA-QDs, suggesting covalent binding of MPA to CdTe QDs surface through thiol group. Circular dichroism (CD) measurements were also performed to assess the optical activity of MPA. According to CD spectra in Fig. 2D, no optical activity was observed for MPA-QDs at 500–600 nm. Thus, the different response patterns of enantiomers in the presence of MPA-QDs are attributed to the intrinsic optical activity of the as-prepared ensemble of CdTe QDs due to the presence of naturally occurring chiral defects [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the interaction of the nanoprobe with enantiomers
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the stereoselectivity of the proposed ratiometric nanoprobe towards L and D enantiomers of Arg and His, the size distribution, surface charge, and their optical activity were measured after the addition of the enantiomers. DLS and ζ-potential measurements were studied to elucidate the substantial difference in fluorescence response patterns of the proposed nanoprobe in the presence of L and D amino acids of interest. DLS result in Fig. S4A shows a considerable increase in the size distribution of MPA-QDs upon the addition of L-Arg. By contrast, the particle size of MPA-QDs remains unaffected in the presence of D-Arg. The surface charge of MPA-QDs also turns to −1.03 mV (Fig. S4B) after the addition of L-Arg, while its enantiomeric counterpart slightly decreases the ζ-potential to −3.78 mV. Similarly, agglomeration of MPA-QDs is observed in the presence of L-His enantiomers (Fig. S4C), whereas aggregation of MPA-QDs in the presence of D-His is negligible. Fig. S4D shows the ζ-potential values of −4.2 mV (D-His) and +1.4 mV (L-His) for MPA-QDs, implying an immense alteration in the surface charge of MPA-QDs in the presence of L-His and D-His enantiomers.

Time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy was employed to further investigate the underlying mechanism. The fluorescence lifetime spectra of MPA-QDs were fitted with an exponential decay function. The average lifetime of the as-prepared MPA-QDs was calculated to be 15.6 ns. The lifetime of the excited state remarkably decreases to 12.0 ns upon the addition of L-Arg, while D-Arg has minimal effect on the decay time (15.2 ns) of MPA-QDs (Fig. S5A). The lifetime of MPA-QDs also declined to 11.3 ns in the presence of L-His, but the decay time of 15.3 ns was observed upon the addition of D-His, which is almost the same as MPA-QDs (Fig. S5B). Bulk properties and surface states contribute to the fluorescence decay of fluorophores [73].

On the other hand, it has been proven that aggregation-induced emission of QDs results in shortened lifetime decay. The smaller the dot-to-dot distance in the aggregation assemblies of QDs, the shorter the lifetime of excited states would be [74]. In this regard, aggregation-enhanced emission of MPA-QDs in the presence of the L-enantiomers of Arg or His seems to play a crucial role in the short-lived lifetime of the excited state of MPA-QDs. Higher concentrations of His or Arg l-amino acids cause a more stacked aggregation state of MPA-QDs, thus a smaller distance between QDs and fluorescence dynamic quenching of the MPA-QDs aggregates. Fluorescence enhancement of MPA-QDs in the presence of D-Arg is attributed to stabilization of MPA-QDs, thus reducing non-radiative recombination of surface defect level electron holes. Regarding the unchanged decay time of MPA-QDs in the presence of D-His, subtle fluorescence quenching of MPA-QDs is highly likely through static pathways.

According to positive cotton effect of l-amino acids in CD spectra (Fig. S6), it is inferred that homochiral interactions between MPA-QDs and L-Arg are more favorable than heterochiral interactions and thus play a prominent role in the distinctive fluorescence response of the nanoprobe.

To better understand the underlying mechanism of stereoselective fluorescence responses for enantiomers, the interactions of Arg and His enantiomers with CdTe (100) surface were calculated using the dispersion-corrected generalized gradient approximation DFT functional (PBE-D3). The details of calculations are reported in supporting information. Fig. 3 displays the different adsorption orientations of enantiomers on the surface, interacting through their active sites. The higher affinity of D-Arg (

In addition, in Fig. 3, the charge density difference map, and the more considerable accumulation of D in contrast to L on the surface, confirmed the trend of interaction energies with higher charge transfer between d-enantiomer and the surface, reducing surface defect of MPA-QDs and thus a slight change in emission wavelength. Due to the higher interaction energies of D-Arg than D-His, an enhancement is also observed in the emission of MPA-QDs.

The red-shift of l-enantiomer for both compounds was recorded in the experimental observation. This observation can be explained by comparing the partial density of state (DOS) for the optimized structures. As shown in Fig. S7a, near the Fermi level, the percent of sp-DOS for both D enantiomers is more than L. Since sp-orbitals for adsorbents have higher energy (in comparison to sp-orbitals of CdTe), the appearance of the experimental peak for D-enantiomers at higher energy (or lower wavelength) with respect to L is explained by the electronic transitions from the molecular orbitals with more sp-character.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and time on recognition of Arg and His enantiomers
ce:section.ce:para: pH plays a leading role on the stability of MPA-QDs. Thus, the emission profiles of MPA-QDs in the presence of phosphate buffer in the pH range of 6.0–9.0 were recorded. Due to fluorescence quenching of MPA-QDs under acidic conditions, their stability was evaluated under neutral and basic conditions. As illustrated in the inset of Fig. S8A, the as-synthesized MPA-QDs with initial pH of 7.0 are stable upon the addition of phosphate buffers with pH range of 6.0–9.0. It has been reported that the interaction of phosphate anions with Cd2+ on the surface of QDs stabilizes the MPA-QDs [75,76]. On the other hand, the target amino acids have pH-dependent protonation states, controlling their interaction with MPA-QDs. The results indicate that the pH exerts a way stronger influence on the interaction of l-amino acids with MPA-QDs than d-amino acids (Figs. S8A–D). MPA with pKa1 = 4.34 is in the form of a carboxylate anion on the surface of QDs, which is inconsistent with the negative surface charge of MPA-QDs. According to acidic constants, protonation of Arg functional groups of guanidine and amine (pKa1COOH = 2.2, pKa2NH2 = 9.0, pKaNH = 12.5) at pH range of 6.0–9.0 facilitates electrostatic interaction between Arg and MPA-QDs. As shown in Fig. S8E, there is a slight difference in fluorescence intensity of MPA-QDs in the presence of either L-Arg or D-Arg enantiomers; however, the L-Arg-induced red-shift in emission spectra of MPA-QDs is remarkably prohibited by the addition of phosphate buffer at different pH values. Fig. S8F depicts the fluorescence intensity ratio of the nanoprobe in the absence and presence of histidine enantiomers. The low fluorescence intensity ratio of the nanoprobe at pH 6.0 is attributed to the deprotonation of the imidazole functional group of His (pKa1COOH = 1.8, pKa2NH2 = 9.2, pKaNH = 6.0), while the higher intensity ratio in the presence of L-His at higher pH values is mainly due to electrostatic interaction of protonated imidazole and amine functional groups with negatively charge MPA-QDs. Furthermore, in the absence of a buffer, the maximum red-shift is observed in the emission spectrum of MPA-QDs. It seems that the competitive interaction of phosphate anions and amino acids with the surface of MPA-QDs favors phosphate anions and thus declines the target-induced red-shift in emission spectra of MPA-QDs. On the other hand, the larger difference between emission wavelength maxima of MPA QDs in the presence D and L amino acids provides a more efficient visual chiral differentiation of enantiomers. In this regard, no buffer was utilized for enantiomeric discrimination of Arg and His.

Fluorescence spectra of the nanoprobe were recorded in the absence and presence of the Arg and His enantiomers in time intervals of 5 min (Figs. S9A–D). The fluorescence intensity ratio of the nanoprobe before and after adding 10 mM D or L enantiomers of Arg and His reaches a plateau in 30 min (Figure S9 E-F). Thus, it was chosen as an optimized time for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the ratiometric nanoprobe in chiral recognition of enantiomers of interest
ce:section.ce:para: To comprehensively study the chiral discrimination performance of the proposed nanoprobe, fluorescence spectra of the combination of BCDs and MPA-QDs were recorded upon the addition of different concentrations of L-Arg and D-Arg enantiomers under optimized conditions. Fig. 4 A and B illustrate the emission color of the proposed ratiometric design in the presence of 0.1–40 mM of D-Arg and L-Arg, respectively. There is a wide color tonality from green to yellow to peach to magenta by increasing the concentration of L-Arg; conversely, the emission color of the nanoprobe slightly changes from green to goldenrod upon the addition of different concentrations of D-Arg. The color alteration of the ratiometric design is in agreement with the fluorescence spectra of the probe in the presence of various concentrations of the D-Arg and L-Arg enantiomers (Fig. 4 C, D). There is a fluorescence enhancement as well as a red-shift from 590 to 610 nm in emission spectra of MPA-QDs in the range of 0.1–12 mM of L-Arg. The higher concentrations of L-Arg result in further red-shift to 618 nm in fluorescence spectra of the MPA-QDs; there is a downward trend in emission intensity, though. In the case of D-Arg, a gradual fluorescence enhancement is observed by increasing D-Arg concentration in the range of 0.1–9 mM, while higher concentrations have led to a decline in fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe.

Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 4E and F, there exist excellent linear relationships between the ratiometric fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe (Imax/I440: Imax refers to maximum emission intensity of MPA-QDs at different concentrations and I440 attributed to emission intensity of BCDs at 440 nm) and the enantiomeric concentration in a certain concentration range. The emission intensity of BCDs remains unchanged in the presence of both D-Arg and L-Arg enantiomers, providing an internal reference to correct analyte independent factors and color tonalities as well. The satisfactory linear relationships and corresponding limits of detection (LODs) of 0.015 and 0.008 mM (3Sblank/m) pertaining to D-Arg and L-Arg summarized in Table 1 indicate the potential applicability of the current ratiometric design for chiral discrimination as well as detection of the enantiomeric percentage of His and Arg in racemic mixtures.

The sensitivity of the current design towards the differentiation of histidine enantiomers was also investigated. The ratiometric nanoprobe response patterns for His enantiomers are comparable to those for Arg, with the exception that the fluorescence signal of MPA-QDs decreases as the concentration of D-His increases (Fig. 5 C). In other words, both have minimal effect on the fluorescence signal of MPA-QDs; however, despite fluorescence enhancement of MPA-QDs in the presence of D-Arg, herein, fluorescence quenching of MPA-QDs is observed by increasing the concentration of D-His. In addition, as shown in Fig. 5D, there is a gradual red-shift from 590 nm to 610 nm by increasing the concentration of L-His enantiomer, together with successive enhancement and quenching in fluorescence emission of MPA-QDs at the concentration range of 0.2–0.75 and 0.9–20 mM respectively. Fluorescence quenching of BCDs in the presence of L-His produces distinctive emission color patterns by which chiral discrimination of His enantiomers and differentiation of L-His from L-Arg are feasible. The emission color of the ratiometric nanoprobe changes from green to yellow to orange and to brick red in the presence of L-His, whereas quenching in green emission of the ratiometric probe is observed upon addition of D-His (Fig. 5A and B). The calibration curve for the determination of D-His in Fig. 5E shows a linear regime within the range of 2–20 mM with a LOD of 0.36 mM. The relationship between fluorescence intensity (Imax/I440) and L-His concentration also shows linear regimes in the ranges of 0.2–75, 1.0–3.0 and 7.5–20 mM with LOD of 0.003 mM based on 3Sblank/m. The detailed analytical figures of merit for the determination of D-His and L-His are summarized in Table 1. Despite numerous chiral recognition protocols relying on chiral selectors, the current ratiometric design with intrinsic chirality of MPA-QDs provides a platform by which chiral sensing of arginine and histidine enantiomers are accomplished. In addition, the merit of the proposed nanoprobe lies in its unique capability of visual tracking of enantiomers.

The capability of the chiral nanoprobe to determine the %ee value in a racemic mixture is highly desirable in terms of its application in the field of food safety and drugs. In this regard, the potential applicability of the proposed ratiometric nanoprobe for the determination of %ee was further evaluated. Interestingly, in contrast to pure L-Arg, there is almost no red-shift in the emission wavelength of MPA-QDs by increasing the percentage of L-Arg in racemic mixtures (Fig. S10A). Meanwhile, there is a negative correlation between intensity ratio of Imax/I440 in the range of 10–90% with equation of y = −0.01 (±3.2E-4) x + 4.87 (±0.02) and R2 = 0.99 (Fig. 6 A). As depicted in Fig. 6A inset, the emission color of the nanoprobe shows an alteration from light yellowish-green to yellowish-orange by increasing the mole fraction of L-Arg from 10 to 90%. The above results indicate the excellent ability of the proposed nanoprobe to recognize L-Arg from the mixture of Arg enantiomers. To further study the behavior of the racemic mixture of Arg enantiomers, fluorescence spectra of the nanoprobe were recorded at different time intervals after the addition of D-Arg to the solution of the nanoprobe containing L-Arg (Fig. S10B). The results show that a red-shift to 604 nm in the emission spectrum of MPA-QDs upon addition of L-Arg, but a competitive interaction between L-Arg and D-Arg towards the MPA-QDs, inhibits aggregation of QDs. This phenomenon can be attributed to the more binding affinity of the surface MPA-QDs to D-Arg than L-Arg, and thus immediate anti-aggregation of MPA-QDs, which is inconsistent with DFT calculations.

In addition, the enantioselectivity of the combination of BCDs/MPA-QDs was evaluated by adjusting the enantiomeric percentage of L-His. Fig. S10C shows the gradual fluorescence enhancement of MPA-QDs by increasing the mole fraction of L-His from 0 to 60%; a further increase in the percentage of L-His results in fluorescence quenching of MPA-QDs. The constant red-shift in the emission spectra of MPA-QDs and the fluorescence quenching of BCDs caused by increasing the fraction of L-His in racemic mixes offer a broad color tonality that allows for enantioselective visual measurement of the mole fraction of L-His in racemic mixtures (Fig. 6B inset). A satisfactory linear relationship between the ratio of fluorescence intensity Imax/I440 and percentage of L-His with the equation of y = 0.01 (±4.7E-4) x + 3.48 (±0.028) with R2 = 0.98 was obtained (Fig. 6B), indicating that the combination of BCDs and chiral MPA-QDs could also be applied for the detection of enantiomeric percentages of His.

Due to the vivid color alteration of the nanoprobe from green to brick red, the potential applicability of the nanoprobe for visual monitoring of enantiomeric excess was also investigated. By increasing %L-His, and thus red-shift in emission spectra of MPA-QDs and decreasing fluorescence emission of BCDs, the contribution of the red value intensity increases (IR), whereas the green values (IG) in the RGB model declines. The relationship between RGB values obtained from the emission color of the ratiometric nanoprobe and %L-His is illustrated in Fig. 6B inset. There exists a linear relationship between IR/IG and %L-His with the equation of y = 0.02 (±0.001) x + 0.78 (±0.0.06) and R2 of 0.97. Despite several optical approaches for recognition of Arg and His, visual tracking of enantiomeric excess using this ratiometric design is a great novelty of the proposed approach. This achievement paves the way for a low-cost, equipment-free chiral discrimination method based on a smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Logic design of the enantioselective ratiometric nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: Visual enantioselective screening of the amino acids of interest has been accomplished by constructing multiple logic gates, including AND, OR, and INHIBIT. Integration of logic gates with nanomaterials has opened new horizons in intelligent diagnosis. Following the laws of Boolean algebra, molecular logic gates transform binary inputs pertaining to chemical signals into binary outputs for the construction of intelligent sensing [77–81].

The proposed ratiometric platform can be used as a portable self-calibrating luminescent logic gate strategy for monitoring the enantiopurity of Arg and His enantiomers. Simple two-to-three logic gates were designed to convert two inputs to three output signals. The presence and absence of L and d-amino acids were defined as “1″ and “0″ as inputs. The high and low (or “off” and “on”) PL intensity of MPA-QDs (IQDs) and BCDs (ICDs), as well as red-shift in emission wavelength (Δλ) as the three outputs, were also encoded with 1's and 0's, respectively. The truth tables for two-to-three logic gates are summarized in Fig. 7 A and B, showing the responses of the ratiometric nanoprobe in different input states. In the absence of the amino acids, there is no change in IQDs, ICDs and Δλ, and thus PASS 0 gate (0,0,0) emerged. PASS 1 gate (1,1,1) is triggered in the presence of L-His even in enantiopure solution or racemic mixture. However, PASS 1 has not appeared in the truth table for arginine. Output signal of (0,1,0) appeared in the presence of enantiopure d-amino acid solutions as well as racemic D/L-Arg combinations. The enantiopure solution of L-Arg also gives a unique output signal (1,1,0). The representative colors are also shown in the truth tables, including L-Arg (pink-red), D-Arg (bright green), L-His (brick-red), and D-His (gray-green). The constructed molecular circuit provides an application prospect in the intelligent analysis of enantiopurity of chiral compounds by fluorescent emission color discrimination.

The chiroselective recognition properties of MPA-QDs in the presence of seven amino acids, including cysteine (Cys), tryptophan (Trp), arginine (Arg), alanine (Ala), histidine (His), proline (Pro) and tyrosine (Tyr) were preliminarily investigated (Fig. S11). The proposed ratiometric design shows stereoselective responses in the presence of Arg and His amino acids, while there is almost no change in the presence of Trp, Ala, Pro and Tyr amino acids. Cysteine enantiomers, though, show fluorescence quenching of MPA-QDs but the fluorescence intensities are almost the same for both enantiomers (Fig. S11A), which makes this platform unsuitable for chiral recognition of cysteine enantiomers. However, previous reports show that TGA-capped CdTe QDs are capable of kinetic discrimination of cysteine enantiomers [48]. In the context of chiral sensing, interference of other chiral species is not a concern, though, since the enantiomeric excess of the two enantiomeric counterparts is a matter of question in pharmaceutics in particular. As in the synthetic route, the coexistence of chiral amino acids other than the target is rare; therefore, different types of amino acids do not count as interference in chiral discrimination. However, these studies have shed light on the sensing mechanism and reasons based on which the proposed probe is capable of chiral discrimination of Arg and His. In Fig. 8 , the response patterns of D and L enantiomers of the seven amino acids are compared in terms of fluorescence intensity and shift in emission wavelength of MPA-QDs. Although a considerable quenching and wavelength shifting in fluorescence emission spectra of MPA-QDs were observed in the presence of cysteine enantiomers, the maximum differentiation between D and L enantiomers are observed only in the presence of arginine and histidine. Regarding the chemical structure of the evaluated amino acids, amine-rich structures of Arg and His seem to play a substantial role in the fluorescence response of the chiral nanoprobe.

The development of chiral sensing platforms is a daunting but delicate challenge in the chemical, food, and pharmaceutical industries. Chiroselective sensing approaches predominantly rely on chiral selectors to differentiate enantiomers of interest. The laborious preparation of chiral selectors is a limiting factor in the pace of developments in this field, though. This work proposes a ratiometric nanoprobe for chiral recognition of arginine and histidine enantiomers by utilizing achiral reagents. The utilization of MPA-QDs without chiral ligands is a great merit of the proposed ratiometric design. Yellow-emissive MPA-QDs with intrinsic chirality and their combination with blue-emissive carbon dots provide a chiroselective ratiometric platform capable of visual tracking of the enantiomers of interest. An increase in the concentration of l-amino acids of histidine and arginine results in successive enhancement and quenching in emission intensity of MPA-QDs, the emission wavelength red-shift towards higher wavelengths. The fluorescence intensity, as well as emission wavelength, are less influenced by d-amino acids, though. Integrating the fluorescence features of the proposed ratiometric nanoprobe with the Boolean logic gate concept, multiple logic gate systems are established with the aim of intelligent monitoring of enantiopurity, which demonstrates the potential of this system to be employed in molecular logic devices at the molecular level for in-field applications. The proposed platform has provided wide color tonalities enabling visual determination of ee% of Arg and His enantiomers. Due to the enantioselective vibrant color changes of the nanoprobe, RGB analysis was applied for the determination of enantiomeric excess in racemic mixture with satisfactory results, enabling onsite visual evaluation of ee (%) using a smartphone. This ratiometric nanoprobe holds a grand promise for rapid, intelligent and valid detection of chiral targets in practical application across various fields, from disease monitoring to drug quality control.

Zahra Jafar-Nezhad Ivrigh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, preparation. Nafiseh Fahimi-Kashani: Conceptualization, Project administration, Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Razieh Morad: Data curation, and, Software. Zahar Jamshidi: Data curation, Software, and, Visualization. Mohammad Reza Hormozi-Nezhad: Supervision, Funding acquisition.
